summary: O'Neil recounts a guest lecture by economist Suresh Naidu explaining the core principles of applied microeconomics: people respond to incentives, supply and demand explain data, and causality matters more than prediction. She notes that economists have been largely unmoved by the big data revolution because big data focuses on description and prediction rather than causality. The post then applies this economic lens to a debate about overseas child labor, showing how economists approach problems by seeking tweakable variables to improve systems. O'Neil concludes with a nuanced acknowledgment that while belief in economic data varies, randomized experiments remain the best way to learn about the world.
key_points: Applied microeconomics rests on three simple metaphors: incentives, supply/demand, and prioritizing causality over prediction
Big data has left microeconomists unmoved because it focuses on description and prediction rather than the causal relationships economists care about
Economists approach problems by trying to identify tweakable variables that can improve systems, as illustrated by differing solutions to child labor
There's a spectrum of how much people 'believe' in economics and supporting data, even among economists themselves
Despite uncertainty, randomized experiments remain the best available method for learning about the world
money_quotes: Causality is more important than prediction.

The real reason they're unmoved, as I infer from his talk yesterday, is that big data almost always focuses on descriptions of human behavior, and sometimes predictions, and almost never causality, which is what economists care about.
A side question: why is it that economists only care about causality? Well they do, and let's take that as a given.
So the economists don't agree, but they all share one goal: to figure out how to tweak a tweakable variable to improve a system.
There's a spectrum of how much people 'believe' in economics, and for that matter believe in data that seems to support a theory or experiment.
It's not clear there's a better way to learn things about the world than doing your best to run randomized experiments.
key_insight: Big data's focus on prediction misses what economists actually care about—understanding causality to identify interventions that can improve systems.
